title: Everyday Physics Myths
description: Thirty-six everyday physics myths and half-truths — does a heavier object really fall faster, why is the sky blue, is there no gravity in space, and does the Coriolis effect spin your sink? Each question pairs the tempting wrong answer with an explanation that teaches the real physics, and every question lists references to Wikipedia or another source so you can verify it and read further.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: Drop a bowling ball and a golf ball at the same instant in a vacuum. Which hits the ground first?

options.text: They land at the same time
The bowling ball, because gravity pulls harder on more mass
The golf ball, because it is more compact
The bowling ball, but only by a tiny, unmeasurable amount

questions.explanation: In a vacuum there is no air resistance, so both objects accelerate downward at the same `g ≈ 9.8 m/s^2` and land together. Gravity *does* pull harder on the heavier ball, but that larger force acts on a proportionally larger mass, so the acceleration `a = F/m` comes out identical — the mass cancels. This equivalence of gravitational and inertial mass is what Galileo argued against Aristotle and what the Apollo 15 crew demonstrated on the airless Moon.

questions.title: A feather really does drift down more slowly than a hammer in ordinary air. What is the actual reason?
questions.description: This is the half-truth hiding inside the 'heavier falls faster' myth.

options.text: Air resistance affects the feather far more relative to its tiny weight
Earth's gravity is genuinely weaker on lighter objects
The feather is gently repelled by the ground
Lighter objects always fall more slowly, even in a vacuum

questions.explanation: The upward drag force depends on an object's size, shape, and speed — not on its weight. A feather has a large surface area but almost no mass, so drag balances its weight almost instantly at a crawling speed (its terminal velocity). A dense hammer must reach a far higher speed before drag catches up. Remove the air and the two fall identically. So the cause is aerodynamic drag, not any difference in how gravity treats them.

questions.title: On Apollo 15 in 1971, astronaut David Scott dropped a hammer and a falcon feather together on the Moon. What happened?

options.text: They struck the surface at the same instant — no air, no air resistance
The hammer landed well before the feather, just like on Earth
The feather floated upward and never landed
Both drifted sideways because of the low gravity

questions.explanation: With essentially no atmosphere on the Moon there is no air resistance, so gravity is the only force and both objects accelerate together at the Moon's `~1.62 m/s^2`, landing at the same moment — exactly as Galileo predicted. It is the cleanest real-world demonstration that, once you remove the air, mass has no effect on how fast something falls.

questions.title: Why is a clear daytime sky blue?

options.text: Air molecules scatter short-wavelength (blue) sunlight much more than long-wavelength (red) light
The sky reflects the blue of the oceans
The atmosphere is made of a faintly blue gas
Sunlight is already blue before it reaches the atmosphere

questions.explanation: This is Rayleigh scattering: tiny air molecules scatter light far more strongly at short wavelengths (the effect grows like `1/wavelength^4`), so blue is redirected all across the sky while red mostly passes straight through. The ocean-reflection idea fails the simplest test — the sky is just as blue over a landlocked desert. (The sea looks blue for its own reasons, including scattering and absorption of light by water.)

questions.title: Violet light is scattered even more strongly than blue. So why doesn't the sky look violet?

options.text: Sunlight contains less violet, and our eyes are far less sensitive to it
Violet light is too heavy to be scattered
All the violet light bounces back into space before reaching us
Sunlight contains no violet at all

questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering really is stronger for violet, so this is a genuine half-truth. But the Sun emits less power at violet wavelengths than at blue, the human eye's colour receptors respond only weakly to violet, and some violet is absorbed high in the atmosphere. The blend of light our eyes actually receive adds up to blue, not violet.

questions.title: What mainly turns the Sun and sky red and orange at sunset?

options.text: The light travels through much more air, so nearly all the blue is scattered away
Dust and pollution are the primary cause of the colour
The Sun genuinely emits redder light in the evening
The cooler evening air changes the colour of the sunlight

questions.explanation: Near the horizon, sunlight skims a long, slanted path through the atmosphere, so Rayleigh scattering strips out most of the blue and green and leaves the reds and oranges to reach your eye — the same physics that makes the midday sky blue. Aerosols, dust, and pollution can deepen or tint a sunset, but they are not the fundamental cause: clean-air sunsets are still red.

questions.title: What colour is sunlight, viewed from space with no atmosphere in the way?

options.text: Essentially white
Yellow
Orange
Pale green

questions.explanation: The Sun radiates across the entire visible spectrum, and that combination is white — which is how astronauts and cameras above the atmosphere see it. Curiously, its emission curve actually peaks in the green, yet we never perceive a green Sun, because all the colours together read as white. It only looks yellow, orange, or red once the atmosphere scatters away some blue, an effect strongest near the horizon.

questions.title: What is the name of the wavelength-dependent light scattering (named after a 19th-century physicist) that makes the sky blue?
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering is the scattering of light by particles much smaller than its wavelength — here, the molecules of the air. Its strength rises steeply as wavelength shrinks (roughly `1/wavelength^4`), so blue light scatters far more than red, painting the whole sky blue. It is named after Lord Rayleigh, who worked it out in the 1870s. Scattering by larger particles (Mie scattering, e.g. cloud droplets) is nearly colour-blind, which is why clouds look white.

questions.title: Which statements about astronauts floating on the International Space Station are TRUE?
questions.description: Select every statement that is correct.

options.text: They are weightless because the station and everyone aboard are in continuous free fall
At that altitude they still feel roughly 90% of Earth's surface gravity
There is essentially no gravity that far from Earth
They float because they have travelled beyond Earth's gravitational pull

questions.explanation: The ISS orbits at about 400 km, where gravity is still roughly `8.7 m/s^2` — about 90% of its value at the surface. Astronauts feel weightless not because gravity is absent but because they are perpetually falling toward Earth while moving sideways fast enough to keep missing it. That is exactly what an orbit is, and 'microgravity' is just sustained free fall.

questions.title: How strong is gravity on the Moon's surface compared with Earth's?

options.text: About one-sixth of Earth's
Exactly zero — the Moon has no gravity
About half of Earth's
The same as Earth's

questions.explanation: The Moon's surface gravity is about `1.62 m/s^2`, against Earth's `9.81 m/s^2` — close to one-sixth, which is why Apollo astronauts bounded across the surface. Any object with mass produces gravity; the Moon simply has far less mass than Earth. The 'no gravity on the Moon' idea confuses weaker gravity with none.

questions.title: You travel from Earth to the Moon. What happens to your mass and your weight?

options.text: Mass stays the same; weight drops to about one-sixth
Both mass and weight stay the same
Both drop to about one-sixth
Mass drops to one-sixth; weight stays the same

questions.explanation: Mass (measured in kilograms) is the amount of matter in you and never changes with location. Weight is the gravitational force on that mass, `weight = mass × g`, so it shrinks to about one-sixth on the Moon, where `g` is one-sixth. Your bathroom scale, which reads weight, would show less — but you would contain exactly as much 'stuff', and shoving you sideways (your inertia) would take just as much effort.

questions.title: What causes Earth's seasons?

options.text: The tilt of Earth's axis, which changes how directly sunlight strikes each hemisphere
Earth's changing distance from the Sun through the year
The Sun growing hotter and cooler over the year
Seasonal changes in the thickness of the atmosphere

questions.explanation: Earth's axis is tilted about 23.4°. The hemisphere leaning toward the Sun receives more direct rays and longer days — summer — while the other hemisphere has winter at the same moment, which already rules out distance as the cause. In fact Earth is *closest* to the Sun (perihelion) in early January, during Northern-Hemisphere winter.

questions.title: Earth's axis is tilted from vertical (relative to its orbit) by about how many degrees? Enter a number.
questions.explanation: Earth's axial tilt (obliquity) is about `23.4°` (more precisely 23.44°). This single number, not any change in distance, is what drives the seasons: it sets how directly each hemisphere faces the Sun through the year. The tilt drifts slowly between roughly 22.1° and 24.5° over a ~41,000-year cycle. If it were 0°, Earth would have no seasons at all.

questions.title: The Moon's 'dark side' — the hemisphere we never see from Earth — receives how much sunlight?

options.text: Just as much as the near side; it has regular days and nights
None — it is permanently dark
Only light reflected from Earth
About half as much as the near side

questions.explanation: The Moon is tidally locked, so it keeps one face toward Earth — but it still turns relative to the Sun, giving every part of its surface about two weeks of daylight and two weeks of night each month. The hidden hemisphere is correctly the 'far side'; calling it the 'dark side' is the myth, popularised by the album title rather than by astronomy.

questions.title: What would actually happen to an unprotected human suddenly exposed to the vacuum of space?

options.text: They would stay conscious for around 15 seconds, swelling and starved of oxygen — but not explode or freeze instantly
They would explode almost immediately
They would freeze solid within a second or two
Their blood would instantly boil and kill them at once

questions.explanation: Skin and blood vessels hold you together, so you would not burst, and a vacuum is an excellent insulator (no air to carry heat away by conduction or convection), so you would not freeze in seconds. The real dangers are oxygen starvation — you would have roughly 15 seconds of useful consciousness — and ebullism, water vaporising in your tissues and causing swelling. Brief exposure is survivable if you are repressurised quickly; in a 1965 NASA chamber accident a technician stayed conscious about 14 seconds and recovered.

questions.title: Which of these statements about sound are TRUE?
questions.description: Select every statement that is correct.

options.text: Sound needs a medium, so it cannot travel through the vacuum of space
Sound travels faster through water than through air
Sound travels faster through steel than through air
A distant explosion in space would be heard moments after its flash

questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical pressure wave that needs particles to pass the vibration along, so true space battles would be silent — no medium, no sound. It also travels faster in stiffer, denser media: roughly `343 m/s` in air, about `1,480 m/s` in water, and over `5,000 m/s` in steel. So the first three statements hold; only the cinematic 'explosion you can hear in space' is impossible.

questions.title: Most coasts get two high tides a day. Why are there two tidal bulges rather than one?

options.text: The Moon's pull is uneven across Earth: it raises water on the near side and leaves a second bulge on the far side
The Sun makes one bulge and the Moon makes the other
Earth wobbles, sloshing the oceans twice a day
The Moon orbits Earth twice every day

questions.explanation: Tides come from the *difference* in the Moon's gravity across Earth (the tidal force). The near-side water is pulled toward the Moon more than Earth's center is, and Earth's center is pulled more than the far-side water — so relative to the planet, water heaps up on both the near and far sides. Earth then rotates through both bulges each day. The Sun adds a smaller pull that strengthens or weakens the tides (spring and neap tides) but is not what creates the second bulge.

questions.title: A metal spoon and a wooden spoon have sat in the same room for hours. Why does the metal feel colder?

options.text: Metal conducts heat away from your hand much faster, even though both are the same temperature
Metal really is colder than wood at the same room temperature
Wood quietly generates its own heat
Metal radiates cold into your hand

questions.explanation: Both objects have been sitting in the same room, so they are at the same temperature. Metal has a high thermal conductivity, so it pulls heat out of your warmer skin quickly; your nerves sense that rapid loss of heat and report 'cold'. Wood conducts poorly, so it feels neutral. You are feeling heat *flow*, not temperature — which is also why a tiled floor feels colder than a rug at the same temperature.

questions.title: People say 'heat rises.' What is the precise version of that statement?

options.text: Hot fluids such as air and water rise because they are less dense; heat itself can travel in any direction
Heat energy always moves straight upward
Heat can only travel sideways
Heat is lighter than cold and floats

questions.explanation: What actually rises is warm, less-dense fluid lifted by buoyancy — a process called convection. Heat as energy also moves by conduction and by radiation, both of which travel in every direction: a hot stove warms the floor beneath it by radiation, downward. So 'hot air rises' is correct, while 'heat rises' is a loose shorthand that hides the real mechanism.

questions.title: You open a freezer door. What is physically happening at the doorway?

options.text: Heat flows out of the warmer room into the cold freezer — 'cold' is simply a lack of heat
Cold flows out of the freezer into the room
Cold and heat stream past each other in opposite directions
Nothing flows until the temperatures are equal

questions.explanation: There is no substance called 'cold'. Thermal energy always flows from hotter to colder, so heat leaves the warm room and enters the cold freezer, which the compressor then pumps away again. 'Letting the cold in' is really 'letting the heat out' — the same reason a cold drink warms up rather than the room cooling down around it.

questions.title: At the top of a very high mountain, how does water boil compared with at sea level?

options.text: It boils at a lower temperature, so boiled food takes longer to cook
It boils at a higher temperature, cooking food faster
It boils at exactly 100°C everywhere on Earth
It will not boil at all

questions.explanation: Water boils when its vapour pressure equals the surrounding air pressure. Higher up, the air pressure is lower, so boiling begins at a lower temperature — about `71°C` (160°F) on the summit of Everest, versus `100°C` at sea level. The water is genuinely cooler, so cooking by boiling is slower at altitude, which is exactly why high-altitude recipes and pressure cookers exist.

questions.title: Why does breathing helium make your voice sound squeaky?

options.text: Sound travels faster in helium, which shifts the resonances (formants) of your vocal tract upward
Helium physically tightens your vocal cords
Helium raises the pitch (fundamental frequency) produced by your vocal cords
Helium makes your vocal cords vibrate far faster

questions.explanation: Your vocal folds vibrate at almost the same fundamental frequency in helium, so the underlying pitch barely changes. Helium is much less dense than air, so sound travels through it about three times faster, which raises the resonant frequencies (the formants) of your vocal tract that shape the *timbre* of your voice — producing the cartoonish quality. A dense gas like sulfur hexafluoride does the opposite, deepening the voice.

questions.title: Is it true that hot water always freezes faster than cold water?

options.text: No — under some conditions hot water has frozen first (the Mpemba effect), but it is unreliable and still debated
Yes — hot water always freezes faster; it is a basic law
No — it is physically impossible for hot water ever to freeze first
Yes, but only in a freezer set below -50°C

questions.explanation: The Mpemba effect — occasional reports of warmer water freezing sooner than cooler water — has been observed in particular setups, with proposed causes including evaporation, dissolved gases, convection currents, supercooling, and the container. But it is not reliably reproducible and has no settled explanation; a 2016 study even questioned whether it is a single, well-defined effect at all. So the sweeping rule 'hot freezes faster' is a myth, even though the phenomenon is a real and active puzzle.

questions.title: Does water conduct electricity?

options.text: Pure water is actually a poor conductor; it is the dissolved ions in ordinary water that carry the current
All water is an excellent conductor because of its molecules
Water never conducts electricity under any conditions
Only hot water conducts electricity

questions.explanation: Ultrapure, deionised water has very high resistivity — it is nearly an insulator — because electrical conduction needs free charge carriers, and in water those are dissolved ions. Tap, sea, and bath water are full of dissolved salts and minerals, so they conduct well. That is the real reason water and electricity are dangerous together: it is the impurities, not the H2O itself, doing the conducting.

questions.title: Why are you relatively safe from lightning inside a hard-topped car?

options.text: The metal shell routes the current around the outside, like a Faraday cage
The rubber tyres insulate you from the ground
Cars are too small to be struck
The glass windows block the electricity

questions.explanation: A lightning bolt has already jumped kilometres through the air, so a few centimetres of rubber tyre stop nothing. What protects you is the car's metal body acting as a Faraday cage: it carries the current around its outer skin and down to the ground, keeping the electric field inside low. Convertibles, fibreglass shells, and open vehicles do not give this protection.

questions.title: 'Lightning never strikes the same place twice.' Is that true?

options.text: No — tall, isolated objects are struck again and again, often many times a year
Yes — a struck spot is somehow 'used up'
Yes, but only for metal objects
It is impossible for lightning to hit the same spot more than once

questions.explanation: Lightning favours height and a good conducting path to ground, so the same structures are hit over and over — the Empire State Building is struck on the order of two dozen times a year, and tall broadcast towers far more often. A strike does nothing to discourage the next one; if anything, the features that drew the first bolt are still there.

questions.title: A magnetic compass needle points toward...

options.text: Magnetic north, which differs from true (geographic) north by an angle called declination
Exactly the geographic North Pole, everywhere on Earth
Whichever large mountain is nearest
The centre of the Earth

questions.explanation: A compass aligns with Earth's magnetic field; in the horizontal plane that points toward magnetic north, which is offset from the geographic pole by the local magnetic declination — an angle that varies with place and drifts slowly over time. (A nice twist: the magnetic pole in the Arctic that attracts a compass's north end is, in magnet terms, actually a south pole.) Careful navigation always corrects for declination.

questions.title: On a spinning carnival ride you feel flung outward. What is really happening?

options.text: Your inertia keeps you moving straight while the ride pushes you inward; 'centrifugal force' is a fictitious force seen only in the rotating frame
A real outward force, centrifugal force, physically pushes on you
Gravity briefly reverses direction
Friction throws you outward

questions.explanation: The only real force on you is centripetal — directed *inward* — supplied by the wall or seat, constantly bending your natural straight-line motion into a circle. The outward 'centrifugal force' appears only when you do the physics in the rotating reference frame; it is an inertial (fictitious) force, not a push from any object. What you actually feel is the wall pressing inward while your body, obeying inertia, tries to keep going straight.

questions.title: A puck slides across ice and slowly stops. With no friction at all, what would it do?

options.text: Keep moving in a straight line at constant speed forever
Slow down and stop anyway, because motion naturally fades
Speed up on its own
Stop instantly the moment you stop pushing it

questions.explanation: Newton's first law (inertia) says an object keeps its velocity unless a net external force acts on it. Everyday objects stop only because friction and air resistance push back on them; remove those and motion simply continues. Aristotle taught that staying in motion needs a continuous force — an intuition that feels right but is wrong, and that Galileo and Newton overturned.

questions.title: Which popular explanation of how a wing makes lift is actually a myth?

options.text: Air split at the front must rejoin at the back, so the air over the longer top surface has to speed up ('equal transit time')
The wing pushes air downward, and by Newton's third law the air pushes the wing up
Faster airflow over the top goes together with lower pressure there
Newton's third law and Bernoulli's principle are two consistent descriptions of the same lift

questions.explanation: The 'equal transit time' story is the myth: nothing forces the two streams that part at the leading edge to meet again at the trailing edge, and in reality the air over the top arrives *sooner*, not just faster. The air over the top genuinely does move faster at lower pressure (Bernoulli holds), but the actual cause of lift is the wing turning air downward (Newton's third law), a picture equivalent to the circulation-and-Bernoulli description. The other three statements here are all correct.

questions.title: Is it true that 'according to the laws of physics, bumblebees shouldn't be able to fly'?

options.text: No — that claim came from applying fixed-wing aeroplane maths to a flapping insect; their flight is well understood
Yes, bumblebee flight genuinely breaks the laws of physics
Yes, but only because they are unusually heavy
No — bumblebees don't really fly, they only glide

questions.explanation: The myth traces back to a back-of-the-envelope calculation that treated a bee like a rigid, fixed aeroplane wing, which badly underestimates the lift available. Real insects use unsteady aerodynamics — rapidly flapping and rotating wings that shed leading-edge vortices — and generate plenty of lift. Physics explains bumblebee flight perfectly well; it was the over-simplified model, not the bee, that was wrong.

questions.title: Could a penny dropped from the top of a skyscraper kill a person on the pavement below?

options.text: No — it tumbles and is so light that its terminal velocity is far too low to be lethal
Yes — it would strike like a bullet
Yes — it would punch straight through a skull
Only if it happened to land edge-first

questions.explanation: Air resistance caps a falling object's speed at its terminal velocity, which depends on mass and shape. A penny is light and flat, so it flutters and tumbles and tops out at only about `25 mph` (roughly `11 m/s`) — fast enough to sting, but not to break skin or kill. (MythBusters tested exactly this.) Heavier, streamlined objects dropped from height are a genuine danger; a penny simply is not.

questions.title: Old cathedral windows are often thicker at the bottom. Is that because glass slowly flows downward like a liquid?

options.text: No — glass is an amorphous solid; the old panes are uneven because of how they were made
Yes, glass is a very slow-moving liquid at room temperature
Yes, gravity drags the glass down over the centuries
Yes, which is why all old glass visibly sags

questions.explanation: Glass is an amorphous solid, and at room temperature its viscosity is so astronomically high that it does not flow on any human — or even geological — timescale. If it did, ancient Roman glass and old telescope lenses would be visibly deformed, and they are not. Medieval window glass was made by spinning molten discs (crown glass), which came out uneven; glaziers then often set each pane with its thicker edge at the bottom.

questions.title: Are natural diamonds formed from coal?

options.text: No — most form deep in the mantle from carbon, long before land plants (and coal) existed
Yes, coal is squeezed into diamond underground
Yes, all diamonds are metamorphosed coal
Yes, which is why both are made of carbon

questions.explanation: Most natural diamonds crystallised one to three billion years ago, roughly 150 km down in Earth's mantle, from carbon under enormous pressure and heat, and were later carried up by deep volcanic eruptions. Coal forms from plant debris in the shallow crust and only appeared after land plants evolved a few hundred million years ago — far too young and too shallow to be the source. Both are carbon, but the 'coal turns into diamond' story is a myth.

questions.title: Does the Coriolis effect decide which way water spins down your sink or toilet?

options.text: No — at that scale it is negligible; the basin's shape and how the water was already moving dominate
Yes — it always spins one way north of the equator and the other way south
Yes — toilets are engineered around the Coriolis effect
Yes — water cannot drain at all on the equator

questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect is real but extraordinarily weak over the few seconds and few tens of centimetres of a draining sink; the leftover swirl from filling, the shape of the basin, and tiny asymmetries set the direction. It genuinely governs large, long-lived systems — hurricanes rotate counter-clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and clockwise in the Southern — but it has no say over your sink, and careful experiments need rigorously still water to reveal it at all.

questions.title: Do microwave ovens cook food 'from the inside out'?

options.text: No — they heat the outer layers (a few centimetres in), and the centre warms mainly by conduction
Yes, they heat the very centre first
Yes, the waves pass through the surface and deposit their energy only at the core
Yes, which is why the edges always stay cold

questions.explanation: Microwaves (around 12 cm wavelength) penetrate only a couple of centimetres into food, where they make water and other polar molecules jostle and heat up — a process called dielectric heating, not some magical 'resonant frequency' of water. The middle then warms more slowly as that surface heat conducts inward, which is exactly why microwaved food often has a cold centre and why standing time and stirring help.
